name: The Parthenon
shortDescription:
Acropolis, Athens, Greece The Parthenon (Greek: Παρθενών) stands as the crown jewel of the Athenian Acropolis and one of the most
                enduring symbols of ancient Greek civilization. Built
longDescription:
Acropolis, Athens, Greece The Parthenon (Greek: Παρθενών) stands as the crown jewel of the Athenian Acropolis and one of the most
                enduring symbols of ancient Greek civilization. Built between 447 and 432 BCE during the Golden Age of
                Athens under the leadership of Pericles, this Doric temple was dedicated to Athena Parthenos ("Athena
                the Virgin"), patron goddess of Athens. The Parthenon represents the pinnacle of Classical Greek
                architecture and embodies the ideals of democracy, wisdom, and civic virtue that defined Athens at the
                height of its power.
description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.
significance:
Built between 447 and 432 BCE during the Golden Age of
                Athens under the leadership of Pericles, this Doric temple was dedicated to Athena Parthenos ("Athena
                the Virgin"), patron goddess of Athens.

geography: Located on the Acropolis in Athens, Greece. Elevation ~156m above sea level. Doric temple with 46 columns. Built 447-432 BCE. Overlooks the city with commanding view. Sacred precinct with smaller shrines and monuments
relatedEvents: Phidias' creation of the gold and ivory Athena Parthenos
Use as Christian church in Byzantine period
Use as mosque during Ottoman occupation
Modern restoration and UNESCO World Heritage status

keyMyths.title: Athena's Birth
keyMyths.description: Athena, the goddess of wisdom and strategic warfare, was born fully formed from the head of Zeus. This event solidified her importance to Athens and led to the Parthenon being dedicated in her honor as the city's patron deity.

keyMyths.title: The Contest Between Athena and Poseidon
keyMyths.description: Athena and Poseidon competed for the patronage of Athens. Poseidon struck the Acropolis with his trident, creating a saltwater spring, while Athena offered an olive tree, symbolizing peace and prosperity. The Athenians chose Athena, leading to the city and the Parthenon being named in her honor.

keyMyths.title: The Panathenaic Festival
keyMyths.description: The Panathenaic Festival was a major Athenian festival celebrated every four years, involving processions, sacrifices, athletic competitions, and the presentation of a newly woven peplos (robe) to the statue of Athena Polias within the Parthenon, highlighting the city's devotion.

keyMyths.title: The Sculptural Program and Athenian Identity
keyMyths.description: The Parthenon's sculptural program, including the metopes, frieze, and pediments, depicted scenes from Athenian mythology and history, such as the Gigantomachy, the Trojan War, and the Panathenaic procession. These sculptures reinforced Athenian identity, values, and their perceived superiority over other cultures.

keyMyths.title: Erichthonius and the Sacred Serpent
keyMyths.description: Erichthonius, a mythical king of Athens born from the earth and raised by Athena, was associated with the sacred serpent of the Acropolis. This connection highlights the autochthonous origins of the Athenians and the goddess's role as protector of the city's lineage.

sources.title: Hesiod, *Theogony*

sources.title: Pseudo-Apollodorus, *Bibliotheca*

sources.name: Aristotle, *The Athenian Constitution*
sources.description: A historical and political treatise attributed to Aristotle (or his school), describing the Athenian government.
sources.title: Aristotle, *The Athenian Constitution*

sources.name: Pausanias, *Description of Greece*
